original_hypothesis.title: Validator Whitelist Permanently Removable During Extended Pause via removeWhitelistAfterValidatorAfk
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: The function removeWhitelistAfterValidatorAfk() has no whenNotPaused modifier. If the rollup admin pauses the contract for a legitimate operational reason (e.g., security incident, upgrade coordination) lasting longer than VALIDATOR_AFK_BLOCKS (201600 blocks, ~28 days at 12s), any address can call this function to permanently and irreversibly disable the validator whitelist. Once unpaused, all validator-gated functions become permissionless, allowing any entity to stake, create assertions, and participate in the rollup without authorization.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: This is a direct code defect: the pausable guard is missing on a critical state-changing function. During extended maintenance windows, an attacker could exploit this to remove the whitelist before the admin intends.

proof_of_signal.pattern: removeWhitelistAfterValidatorAfk() is external with no whenNotPaused modifier; block.number keeps incrementing during pause, making _validatorIsAfk() return true

preconditions: No new assertions confirmed during the pause

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: removeWhitelistAfterValidatorAfk lacks whenNotPaused while all other state-mutating validator functions include it
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Admin pause for extended maintenance could trigger unintended whitelist removal
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any time the contract is paused for >28 days and no assertions are confirmed

operational_questions: Can the admin re-enable the whitelist after it's been disabled?
Is there a mechanism to extend the AFK threshold during planned maintenance?
What is the longest historical pause duration for any Arbitrum rollup?
validation_requirements: Confirm no admin function exists to re-enable the whitelist once disabled
Test calling removeWhitelistAfterValidatorAfk while contract is paused
Verify block.number advancement during paused state

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Wait for rollup to be paused for operational reasons (security incident, upgrade)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Monitor block.number until VALIDATOR_AFK_BLOCKS pass since last assertion activity
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Call removeWhitelistAfterValidatorAfk() - succeeds even during pause
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Once contract is unpaused, any address can now act as validator
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: None - single transaction by any address

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Permanent irreversible removal of validator whitelist
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Non-whitelisted entities can create assertions and participate in challenges
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: No on-chain recovery mechanism exists to re-enable the whitelist
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All validator-gated functions: stakeOnNewAssertion, confirmAssertion, returnOldDeposit, reduceDeposit, addToDeposit, createLayerZeroEdge

execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Must wait for 201600 blocks of pause duration

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Monitor pause events and block count since last confirmed assertion

validation_result.mechanism: missing whenNotPaused on whitelist removal during pause
validation_result.impact_summary: removeWhitelistAfterValidatorAfk() lacks whenNotPaused modifier, allowing anyone to permanently disable the validator whitelist during an extended contract pause (>28 days), since validators cannot confirm assertions while paused but block.number continues to increment.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The function removeWhitelistAfterValidatorAfk() at RollupUserLogic.sol:73-77 is an external state-changing function that sets validatorWhitelistDisabled = true. It has NO whenNotPaused modifier, unlike every other state-mutating function in the contract. The _validatorIsAfk() check uses block.number which continues to increment during a pause. Meanwhile, confirmAssertion (the only way to advance the latest confirmed assertion) has whenNotPaused, meaning no assertions can be confirmed while paused. This creates a guaranteed path: if the contract is paused for longer than VALIDATOR_AFK_BLOCKS (201600 blocks, ~28 days at 12s blocks), the AFK condition becomes true through no fault of validators, and any address can call removeWhitelistAfterValidatorAfk() to permanently disable the whitelist.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: Direct code evidence confirms: (1) removeWhitelistAfterValidatorAfk at RollupUserLogic.sol:73-77 has no whenNotPaused modifier, (2) confirmAssertion at RollupUserLogic.sol:85 has onlyValidator whenNotPaused, (3) stakeOnNewAssertion at RollupUserLogic.sol:133 has onlyValidator whenNotPaused, (4) _validatorIsAfk at RollupUserLogic.sol:54-62 uses block.number which is unaffected by pause state. The inconsistency is clear: the function that removes the whitelist is callable during pause, while all functions that could prevent the AFK condition are blocked during pause.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: Missing whenNotPaused modifier. This function can be called while the contract is paused, but all functions that could prevent the AFK condition (confirmAssertion, stakeOnNewAssertion) are blocked by whenNotPaused.
Uses block.number which continues incrementing during pause. Since confirmAssertion and stakeOnNewAssertion are paused, the AFK timer will inevitably trigger during any extended pause.
confirmAssertion has whenNotPaused, meaning no assertions can be confirmed during pause, which prevents validators from resetting the AFK timer.

calling_sequence: 2. All whenNotPaused functions are blocked: confirmAssertion, stakeOnNewAssertion, returnOldDeposit, etc.
3. block.number continues incrementing for 201600+ blocks (~28 days)
4. _validatorIsAfk() returns true because latestConfirmedAssertion.createdAtBlock + VALIDATOR_AFK_BLOCKS < block.number
5. Attacker calls removeWhitelistAfterValidatorAfk() - no whenNotPaused check
6. require(!validatorWhitelistDisabled) passes if whitelist was enabled
7. require(_validatorIsAfk()) passes due to elapsed blocks
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The attack path is: pause -> wait 201600 blocks -> call removeWhitelistAfterValidatorAfk(). The key defect is at RollupUserLogic.sol:73 where the function declaration lacks whenNotPaused. Compare with line 85 (confirmAssertion) and line 133 (stakeOnNewAssertion) which both have whenNotPaused. The _validatorIsAfk check at line 54-62 uses block.number at lines 59 and 61, which increments regardless of pause state.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment scripts provided that would contradict this finding. The common.ts config file shows disableValidatorWhitelist as a config setting, confirming the whitelist feature is a deployable configuration.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The attack scenario is valid and realistic. A rollup admin might pause the contract for legitimate reasons (security incident response, coordinated upgrade). If the pause lasts more than 28 days (which is plausible for complex security incidents or governance delays), anyone can call removeWhitelistAfterValidatorAfk() to permanently disable the validator whitelist. This is not the intended behavior - the AFK mechanism is designed to detect validator inactivity, not to trigger during administrative pauses where validators are actively blocked from operating.
validation_result.impact_assessment: HIGH impact. The validator whitelist is a critical access control mechanism that restricts who can participate in the rollup validation. Its permanent removal during a pause means that upon unpausing, any entity can stake, create assertions, and participate in the challenge game without authorization. While creating invalid assertions still requires capital and will be challenged, the removal of the whitelist fundamentally changes the security model from permissioned to permissionless without admin intent.
recommended_mitigations: Add whenNotPaused modifier to removeWhitelistAfterValidatorAfk() to prevent calling during paused state
Alternatively, modify _validatorIsAfk() to account for paused time, e.g., by tracking cumulative pause duration and subtracting it from the elapsed blocks
Add an admin function to re-enable the validator whitelist (set validatorWhitelistDisabled = false) as a recovery mechanism
Consider using a separate pausable mechanism for the AFK timer that the admin can reset when pausing
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy RollupUserLogic behind proxy with validator whitelist enabled. 2. Create genesis assertion. 3. Pause the contract via admin. 4. Advance block.number by 201601 blocks (using hardhat mine). 5. Call removeWhitelistAfterValidatorAfk() from non-validator address. 6. Verify validatorWhitelistDisabled == true. 7. Unpause contract. 8. Verify non-whitelisted address can call onlyValidator functions.
uncertainties: An admin logic contract (not in scope) may have a function to re-enable the whitelist, which would provide a recovery mechanism but would not eliminate the defect
The practical likelihood of a 28-day pause depends on operational practices, though complex security incidents or governance deadlocks could plausibly cause such delays
additional_testing_needed: Check if RollupAdminLogic (not in scope) has a setValidatorWhitelistDisabled function for recovery
Test the exact block timing on different L1/L2 configurations to verify the 28-day assumption
Verify that the proxy admin cannot directly write to the validatorWhitelistDisabled storage slot as an emergency measure
